editorial.title: The great vape vanishing act: a tragedy for 'confectionery' enthusiasts
editorial.teaser: As the government plays whack-a-mole with illicit tobacco shops, professor remy asks the hard questions: without these neon-lit sanctuaries, where will we buy phone chargers that break in three hours and bubblegum-flavoured air?
editorial.body:
It is a somber week for the high street. We are witnessing a purge of biblical proportions—or at least, bureaucratic proportions.

the government, in its infinite wisdom and newfound visual acuity, has decided to crack down on the illicit tobacco and vape trade, shuttering stores with the ferocity of a parent finding a stash of energy drinks in a toddler’s playpen. For years, we have walked past these architectural marvels.

you know the ones. They call themselves "candy shops," yet display a suspicious lack of cadbury.

they brand themselves "gift stores," yet the only gift available is a precipitous nicotine addiction wrapped in chinese plastic. These establishments have been the backbone of our local economy, primarily because they are the only ones able to afford the rent by selling "chop-chop" tobacco out of a hollowed-out photocopier.

but alas, the new laws are here. The authorities have shockingly discovered that the store with blackout curtains, neon signs screaming "vapes," and no visible customers buying milk, might just be bending the rules.

who could have foreseen this? the crackdown is swift.

padlocks are being applied to doors, and millions of dollars in illicit product are being seized. One must admire the government’s optimism.

they truly believe that by closing the physical "convenience store" (convenient only if you need an unmarked pack of cigarettes and a questionable meat pie), they will end the trade. It is a classic game of regulatory whack-a-mole.

we are closing the store on the corner, which means the illicit trade will simply move to the slightly less well-lit store around the corner. But let us take a moment to mourn the aesthetic loss.

without these vibrant, illicit hubs, our streets will just be filled with empty leases and legitimate businesses paying taxes. And really, how boring is that?
editorial.author: Professor Remy
